forecasts.reasoning: Multiple credible search results confirm that Kilmar Abrego Garcia, who was mistakenly deported to El Salvador, was returned to the United States in early June 2025. Specifically, reports from NPR, Al Jazeera, Politico, and CNN published on or around June 6, 2025, state that he was returned to the U.S. (arriving in Nashville, Tennessee) to face criminal smuggling charges. A federal grand jury indicted him on May 21, and Attorney General Pam Bondi announced his return on June 6, 2025. His entry onto U.S. territory occurred well before the July 1, 2025 deadline. The resolution criteria are strictly satisfied.

Based on the evidence gathered, there was no official government announcement of an agreement before the July 1, 2025 deadline. The news reporting clearly established that while negotiations were in their 'final stages' in January 2025, the actual contract was not finalized by government officials until July 2025, and public reporting of the signed agreement (via Yonhap and other outlets) only occurred in mid-August 2025. Thus, the condition of announcing an agreement BEFORE July 1, 2025 was not met.

The Carnegie Global Protest Tracker typically records distinct waves of "significant antigovernment protests", rather than every discrete protest event. Since the 2024-2025 South Korean political crisis was triggered by the December 2024 martial law declaration, the tracker will almost certainly use a December 2024 start date for this protest wave. As the question was launched in early 2025, any entry representing this movement will fall outside the resolution criteria (which strictly requires a start date after the question's launch). The base rate of a completely new, distinct mass anti-government movement (10,000+ participants) breaking out in the short 4-5 month period before July 2025, and being distinctively added as a new entry to the tracker, is quite low given the tracker averages less than a hundred entries per year globally. I estimate a 12% chance for a new, tracker-eligible protest starting in this tight timeframe.

Reports and expert analysis from late 2025 (Nov 2025) and early 2026 (Feb/March 2026) refer to North Korea's *seventh* nuclear test as a future possibility, noting they are "poised" or "ready" to conduct one. If a test had occurred prior to July 1, 2025, these sources would refer to it in the past tense or be discussing an eighth test. Therefore, it is practically certain that no seventh nuclear test occurred before the July 1, 2025 deadline.

The question asks if the US will publicly accuse Russia of invading another country (other than Ukraine) before July 1, 2025. Background text establishes the context of Putin 'going for' another country next. Extensive searching did not uncover any events between April 2024 and July 1, 2025, in which Russia sent ground forces into a new country, nor did it find the U.S. government making any accusations of such an invasion. Speculation about Moldova, the Baltics, or Georgia remains just that—speculation. There was an August 2024 recognition by a US Embassy of the anniversary of the 2008 Georgia invasion, but this does not constitute a new accusation of invasion in the spirit (or typical resolution practice) of questions framed around 'what country will Putin go for next.' There are no unexpected edge cases, making a negative resolution virtually certain.

Multiple credible sources published well after the resolution date of July 1, 2025 confirm that Libya has not ratified the AfCFTA. A February 2026 report from the US International Trade Administration (trade.gov) explicitly states that as of December 2025, 'Benin, Libya, South Sudan, and Sudan still have not ratified it'. Similarly, an update from the highly regarded TRALAC (Trade Law Centre) from January 2026 lists Libya among the very few countries yet to ratify. Because the July 1, 2025 deadline has already elapsed and authoritative post-deadline sources consistently agree that ratification did not occur, the probability of this event occurring is effectively zero.
